## [03:02 - 06:03]

is lacking. So the point I'm making here is this, you have to get your service way up there. The ease of purchase is the way to service somebody. Convenience is the way to service someone, not just a cheaper price. People will pay more for service than they will for the product. You get that? People will pay more for great service than they will for a great product. So how can you create a level of service that separates you from others and that people will pay for? Going to the client, rather than the client coming to you, is a great service. I don't know why more businesses don't do this, okay? Hey, I know you want to look at this, but what about this and this as well? ## [06:03 - 08:53]

The clientele, the people that I'm going to be around, the people my wife is going to be around, the people my kids are going to be around. At $400 a night, I'm going to be around players, right? I'm not going to be around people that are at my level but above my level. That's who I want my daughter around. That's who I want my wife around. And that's who I want to be around. Hey, man, that's who I want photographs of. That's who I want memories with. So what did I get for the discount? Oh, I saved $360 a night. Big deal. That's the end of your lifetime. What did the customer get when you drop your price $1,000 or $10,000 or a million? What did they get? Oh, now they can afford it. You can't use a discount. You don't have to look far to find companies. I mean literally just hundreds of companies that are known for great service and higher prices. But I can show you thousands of companies that are known for lower service and lower prices and that will be out of business. People even brag when they pay extra to do business at the Tiffany's, the Ritz, the Four Seasons. People pay $2,500 a year to own the black card to get the Express and Centurion service from American Express. Look, I want you to elevate your level of service to that black card. I want you to elevate your level of service to the Four Seasons. I don't want you to be competing with your competitor. I want you to get way above them. That's what you want. I want you to put your customer on a level of service where they don't shop you. They don't mention price. They just like, okay, let's do it because I know you're going to service me. Take care of me. How much are you willing to bend over backwards to make sure your customer's happy? You know, I attended a seminar once and there was a salesperson there and they asked the speaker because it was on telephones and they asked the speaker, hey, when I'm calling back a client, you know, what if there's a problem with the product? And the individual running the seminar said, look, you should never call a client and ask for a problem. Well, there was, I don't know, a couple hundred people in the room and they're all like, oh, that's good. They're taking notes. I should never ask for a problem on a phone call. And I'm sitting there thinking, no, man, you want problems. Problems are opportunities. Look, if somebody's got a problem and you don't service the problem, you don't know about it. In my office, we have a policy in place. All complaints come straight to me. I want the complaint. I want to know about them. Why? These are opportunities for me to service, for me to separate myself. Look, it's not the service department's problem. It's not the call center's problem. It's the customer's problem. And every problem a customer has, you want to take care of. This gives you an opportunity to have a relationship, to own that client. Okay? So, service is senior to selling. Look for every opportunity possible to service your client.
